column1: #tags:squirrelops-enablement
What is SquirrelOps in one sentence?
column2: A deception security platform for AI. It sits in front of your customer-facing LLM, routes attackers into a decoy, and sends labeled threat intel to your SIEM.

column1: What does SquirrelOps do that guardrails don't?
column2: Guardrails block prompts at the door. SquirrelOps catches the attacker who gets through, routes them to a decoy, and tells you how they got in.

column1: What is the core value proposition?
column2: Most LLM security keeps attackers out. SquirrelOps makes them reveal themselves. Detection and attribution for the LLM attack surface.

column1: What is the 30-second pitch?
column2: Most LLM security tries to keep attackers out. We make them reveal themselves. We sit in front of your LLM, route attackers to a decoy, capture what they tried, and send labeled intel to your SIEM. Real model stays clean.

column1: What category is SquirrelOps?
column2: Deception for the LLM attack surface. The intersection of network deception and AI security. A new category, effectively uncontested commercially.

column1: What is the threat capture rate and what does it mean?
column2: 98.5%, meaning 66 of 67 attack turns captured in the v1.0.4 internal adversarial campaign.

column1: Why must you say "of attack turns" with the 98.5%?
column2: The campaign is 87 total turns; 67 are attacks, 20 benign. 98.5% is 66 of 67 attacks. The raw canary rate across all 87 is 75.86%. Pairing 98.5% with 87 is the trap.

column1: What do you do when asked "98.5% of what?"
column2: Say 66 of 67 attack turns, then volunteer that the raw rate across all 87 turns is ~76%, and explain the attack-turn number is the meaningful one.

column1: What is the false positive rate?
column2: Zero on the benign traffic test set. No production numbers yet (pre-customer). Never claim "zero in production."

column1: What is the detection time?
column2: Under 2 minutes from the first malicious request to alert.

column1: How much latency does SquirrelOps add?
column2: About 30ms on benign (clean) traffic, below the noise floor of LLM response times.

column1: How many tests does SquirrelOps have?
column2: 815 on the v1.0.5 release branch; over 1,000 across the whole suite.

column1: How many attack techniques does the campaign cover?
column2: 6 technique families across 7 attacker profiles, roughly 120 prompts. Never say "80+ techniques."

column1: What are the 6 attack technique families?
column2: DAN-style jailbreaks, role-play injection, system-prompt extraction, encoding evasion, tool-call exploitation, and multi-step chains.

column1: What ML model does Layer 2 use?
column2: A 700MB DeBERTa-v3 model exported to ONNX, shipped inside the profile bundle.

column1: What is the two-layer detection pipeline?
column2: Layer 1 deterministic regex rules (sub-ms), then Layer 2 ML classifier (DeBERTa-v3 ONNX, 10-30ms). Scores combine via max.

column1: What happens to adversarial traffic?
column2: It gets routed to a decoy persona model. The real model never sees the attacker.

column1: What happens to benign traffic?
column2: It passes through to the real model untouched, adding ~30ms.

column1: What is the decoy persona model?
column2: A LoRA-adapted small open-weight model trained to engage attackers convincingly without leaking real secrets.

column1: What is a profile bundle?
column2: The signed, reproducible artifact shipping a tenant's rules, ML model, and decoy persona. Customers can rebuild it from source and verify bit-identical.

column1: What does "bit-identical reproducible build" mean and what is its scope?
column2: Customers rebuild a profile bundle from source and verify it byte-for-byte matches the deployed one. Scope is the bundle pipeline (HueyDeweyLouie), not the control-plane core.

column1: How is intel emitted?
column2: STIX 2.1 over TAXII2. Standard format, polled by the customer's SIEM or threat-intel platform.

column1: What are the TAXII2 endpoints?
column2: /taxii2/ discovery, /taxii2/api/ root, /taxii2/api/collections, and /taxii2/api/collections/{id}/objects.

column1: What STIX object types are emitted?
column2: Indicators (IP, UA, session token), attack-patterns (techniques), and relationships, enriched with ATT&CK and ATLAS.

column1: What are the three deployment topologies?
column2: In-line gateway, sidecar, and VPC-peered / isolated. All produce identical intel output.

column1: Which SIEM integration is documented?
column2: OpenCTI, via its TAXII2 connector. Others (Splunk, Sentinel) are standard "point your TAXII2 collector at our feed."

column1: What does the customer provide to deploy?
column2: A place to run it, traffic routing, a TAXII2-capable SIEM or bridge target, the in-scope models/use cases, and a security contact.

column1: What is the honest limit on SIEM connectors?
column2: Standard TAXII2 plus a documented OpenCTI path. No universal one-click connector. Custom targets are scoped bridges.

column1: What does CLOWNPEANUTS_REQUIRE_STAGE2 do?
column2: Makes the runtime fail closed (raise) if a bundle is supposed to ship the ML model but it's missing, instead of silently running rules-only.

column1: Name the seven modules.
column2: HueyDeweyLouie (AI Deception), FunHouseForge, GhostCrew, WitchBait, ADLibs, PripyatSprings, DirtyLaundry.

column1: What is HueyDeweyLouie?
column2: The AI Deception module (v1.0.4 headline). LoRA decoy, two-layer detection, signed reproducible bundles. The deepest, most complete module.

column1: What is FunHouseForge?
column2: The decoy lifecycle orchestrator. Stands up, activates, and tears down deception environments. The hub the other modules hang off.

column1: What is GhostCrew?
column2: Synthetic activity generator. Makes decoys look alive with realistic SSH/HTTP/DB activity so attackers can't spot them as empty.

column1: What is WitchBait?
column2: Credential canary system. Plants trackable fake credentials and records when one is used.

column1: What is the honest line on ADLibs?
column2: It generates Active Directory deception objects and the detection around them today. Live LDAP bind into production AD is deployment scoping, not turnkey yet.

column1: What is the honest line on PripyatSprings?
column2: It maintains the artifact fingerprint registry and callback tracking. It does not yet generate the beaconing documents themselves.

column1: What is DirtyLaundry?
column2: Adversary behavioral profiling. Five weighted metrics classify an actor from script-kiddie to APT, exported as native + STIX.

column1: What is ClownPeanuts?
column2: The runtime engine. Service emulators, the rabbit-hole engine, the detection trap layer, and the STIX/TAXII2 server. The substance of the product.

column1: What is the honest framing of the SquirrelOps core?
column2: A control plane and aggregator, not a heavyweight brain. The intelligence lives in ClownPeanuts and the AI module.

column1: Who are the five buyer personas?
column2: CISO, GRC/AI Governance, Head of AppSec, SOC Manager, Head of AI Platform.

column1: Who are the decision-makers vs evaluators?
column2: Decision-makers: CISO, GRC, sometimes AppSec. Evaluators (can veto): SOC Manager, AI Platform lead.

column1: Who is the silent veto?
column2: The Head of AI Platform. They can quietly kill a deal by telling the CISO it adds latency/risk. Win them early.

column1: What do you lead with for a CISO?
column2: The detection-gap story: you have a Protect layer for AI, but what's in the Detect column for your LLMs?

column1: What do you lead with for a GRC buyer?
column2: The honest framework framing: we slot into Detect/Respond of what you already run, and we publish a quantified number.

column1: What do you lead with for AppSec?
column2: Architecture and fit: the four layers, ~30ms latency, STIX/TAXII2 into their stack, reproducible bundles they can audit.

column1: What do you lead with for a SOC Manager?
column2: Signal quality: zero false positives, every alert a real adversarial session with attribution, into the SIEM they already run.

column1: What do you lead with for AI Platform lead?
column2: Minimal footprint: ~30ms, loads once, benign passes through, real model never sees attackers. "We're a guest, not a tenant."

column1: How do you sense which persona you're talking to?
column2: By the first thing they probe: risk/board (CISO), frameworks (GRC), the app (AppSec), alerts/noise (SOC), latency/the model (AI Platform).

column1: Is the LLM-deception category contested?
column2: No commercial product does it. It's been academic research (Palisade's LLM Agent Honeypot). SquirrelOps defines the category.

column1: What is the IP caution on the category?
column2: A US patent (12423441) exists on generative-LLM deception. Don't claim "first/only" publicly until counsel reviews it. Say "no commercial product we've found."

column1: How is SquirrelOps different from Lakera?
column2: Lakera is an API filter (now Check Point). It blocks at the door and assumes the block holds. We catch the attacker who beats it.

column1: How is SquirrelOps different from Acalvio?
column2: Acalvio makes a honeypot WITH AI (LLM generates fake servers). SquirrelOps makes a honeypot that IS an AI (decoy model catching LLM attacks).

column1: How is SquirrelOps different from HiddenLayer / red-teaming?
column2: They test and harden your model before deployment. We catch the attacker after deployment, in production. Complementary.

column1: What happened with the AI guardrail vendors?
column2: Consolidated: Lakera to Check Point, Prompt Security to SentinelOne, Protect AI to Palo Alto, Robust Intelligence to Cisco.

column1: What is the EchoLeak proof point?
column2: CVE-2025-32711, June 2025. Zero-click prompt injection in M365 Copilot that beat Microsoft's own classifier. Proof that filters fail.

column1: What is the real TAM figure to cite?
column2: Agentic AI security: $1.65B in 2026 growing to $13.5B by 2032, ~42% CAGR (MarketsandMarkets).

column1: What market gap stat can you cite?
column2: 47% of organizations have no AI-specific security controls (Kiteworks 2025).

column1: Which stats must you NOT cite?
column2: Prompt injection up 340% YoY and "88% had an AI-agent incident." Vendor-blog noise, not primary research.

column1: What is the one-sentence competitive positioning?
column2: Everyone else builds a taller wall around your LLM. We build the room on the other side, where the attacker reveals their playbook.

column1: What are the three pricing tiers?
column2: Pilot ($50-75K), Mission ($150-250K/yr + impl), Constellation ($400K-$1M+/yr).

column1: What is the Pilot tier?
column2: $50-75K fixed, 4-6 weeks, single endpoint, credits 100% toward year one.

column1: What is the Mission tier?
column2: $150-250K/yr platform plus $50-100K implementation. Mid-market, 1-3 endpoints, ~10M queries/mo.

column1: What is the Constellation tier?
column2: $400K-$1M+/yr. Multi-BU, multi-environment, air-gap, dedicated TAM, custom rule packs.

column1: What is the core anchoring rule?
column2: Anchor high, quote list, let procurement discount you. Founders undercharge by 40-60% in early enterprise sales.

column1: How should you NOT price?
column2: Per query. It creates buyer anxiety. Use platform plus envelope.

column1: How do you handle "this is expensive"?
column2: Compared to what? Let them anchor first. Usually they compare to a filter; reframe to deception pricing.

column1: How do you handle "we'll build it ourselves"?
column2: 12-18 months of engineering: two-layer pipeline, decoy model, signed bundles, 800-test gate, STIX pipeline. What's that worth, plus exposure while you build?

column1: What is the lighthouse pricing lever?
column2: First 5 customers get 30-50% off in exchange for case study, reference rights, and co-marketing.

column1: What are the three pressure reflexes for objections?
column2: Pause then answer in two sentences and stop; agree with the true part first then pivot; if you don't know, defer with a date and never invent.

column1: How do you handle "you have no customers"?
column2: Agree: lighthouse phase. Turn it: our attention, our roadmap, lighthouse pricing. In a year you're one of fifty. Price it as access.

column1: How do you handle "isn't this just a honeypot?"
column2: Same proven idea, new surface. Network honeypots are a decade-old trusted category. Ours engages attackers and emits intel; it doesn't just sit there.

column1: How do you handle "what if the attacker realizes it's a decoy?"
column2: By then they've revealed technique, tooling, and intent. That's the win. GhostCrew also keeps decoys from looking empty.

column1: How do you handle "does it see our real data?"
column2: The decoy isn't wired to real systems, so it leaks nothing. Benign traffic passes to your model. Deploy in a VPC you control for residency.

column1: How do you handle "are you SOC 2 / FedRAMP certified?"
column2: Not yet, and I won't pretend. We support your program with evidence and can produce a control-mapping package as an engagement.

column1: How do you handle "why trust an early vendor?"
column2: Because of what deploys, not who we are. Signed, reproducible bundles you verify yourself. Source-available runtime. Standard STIX. No black box.

column1: What is the "I don't know" script?
column2: Good question, I don't want to guess. What I can tell you is [closest thing]. Let me get you the precise answer by [day]. Then follow up.

column1: What framework framing do you open with for GRC?
column2: AI Deception isn't its own framework. It slots into the Detect and Respond functions of whatever you already run.

column1: Which NIST CSF controls does SquirrelOps map to?
column2: DE.CM and DE.AE (Detect), RS.AN and RS.CO (Respond).

column1: Which CIS Control is the gift, and why?
column2: Control 13 (Network Monitoring and Defense). It explicitly calls for deception technology. The framework already asked; we answer it for AI.

column1: Which NIST AI RMF functions does SquirrelOps support?
column2: Measure and Manage. The quantified metrics exist because the framework asks for evidenced AI risk measurement.

column1: Which OWASP LLM Top 10 items get captured?
column2: LLM01 Prompt Injection, LLM06 Sensitive Info Disclosure, and adjacent items, categorized at the decoy.

column1: What compliance must you NOT claim?
column2: SOC 2 Type II, FedRAMP authorization, ISO 27001/42001 certification. These are buyer obligations we support, not vendor certs.

column1: What is the pilot delivery team and cost?
column2: ~228 person-hours over 6 weeks across founder/SE, lead engineer, and a 1099 threat researcher. ~$51K COGS.

column1: What gates pilot throughput?
column2: The lead-engineer role is near-full-time per pilot. One engineer is ~5-6 pilots/year; two is ~10-12.

column1: What is the partnership posture?
column2: Complement, not compete. We produce threat intel; SIEMs/TIPs/AI platforms consume it. We extend partners on the LLM surface.

column1: What is the partnership mental model?
column2: You produce, they consume. SquirrelOps is the sensor; the partner's platform is the workbench/pipeline.

column1: How do you turn "you're early" into a partnership advantage?
column2: A big vendor makes a partner wait in line. We co-build the integration in weeks, their way, and they get a differentiated AI security story first.

column1: What is the Flowsint-style integration pattern?
column2: A SquirrelOps enricher the partner's tool calls to pivot from an attacker fingerprint into our capture data. Sensor to workbench.

column1: What should you never do in a partnership call?
column2: Position as a competitor, oversell the installed base, or commit to roadmap you can't ship.

column1: What is the deployment timeline by tier?
column2: Pilot 4-6 weeks (one endpoint), Mission 6-8 weeks (production), Constellation 8-12 weeks (white-glove, multi-env).

column1: What is the honest line on the auth default?
column2: The intel feed can run unauthenticated for local use. A real deployment turns auth on. We added a loud startup warning when it's off.

column1: Why is the zero-FP claim structurally strong?
column2: A legitimate user never talks to a decoy. If something is in the decoy, it's hostile by construction. That's why the FP number is what it is.

column1: What is the supply-chain story?
column2: Every bundle is Ed25519-signed and bit-identically reproducible. The customer rebuilds from source and verifies, no trust in our infrastructure required.

column1: What is SquirrelOps Home and is it enterprise?
column2: A consumer macOS network-security app under a Noncommercial license. Not enterprise. Exclude it from enterprise positioning.
